perturbation.original_text: JANA Partners LLC ("JANA") and URS Corporation (the "Company"). In consideration of and reliance upon the mutual covenants and agreements contained herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: JANA Partners LLC ("JANA") and URS Corporation (the "Company"). In consideration of and reliance upon the mutual covenants and agreements contained herein, and for other nominal consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto agree as follows:
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'good and valuable consideration' to 'other nominal consideration' introduces uncertainty and contradicts general contract law principles. Adequate consideration is necessary for a binding contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law requires 'consideration' to be something of value. 'Nominal consideration' implies a lack of substantial exchange, potentially voiding the agreement depending on jurisdictional interpretation of adequacy of consideration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 'good and valuable consideration' to 'nominal consideration' directly contradicts the legal requirement for adequate consideration in contract law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that consideration must be something of value for a contract to be enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable.' Replacing 'good and valuable consideration' with 'nominal consideration' implies that the exchange lacks substantial value, which could invalidate the contract. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by undermining a core requirement for contract validity.

perturbation.original_text: During the Cooperation Period, JANA shall cause all shares of the Company's capital stock ("Shares") beneficially owned, directly or indirectly, by it, or by any of its Affiliates or Associates (including without limitation all Shares beneficially owned as of the respective record dates for the 2014 Annual Meeting and as of the record dates for any special meeting of shareholders) over which it exercises or has voting authority, to be present for quorum purposes and to be voted, at such meetings or at any adjournments or postponements thereof, in favor of the current members of the Board (including the JANA Nominees) that will be up for election at such meetings, and not to submit any proposal for consideration at, or bring any other business before, the 2014 Annual Meeting or initiate, encourage or participate in any "withhold" or similar campaign with respect to the election of directors at the 2014 Annual Meeting and shall not permit any of its Affiliates or Associates to do any of the foregoing or publicly or privately encourage or support any other stockholder to take any such actions.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Cooperation Period, JANA shall cause all shares of the Company's capital stock ("Shares") nominally owned, directly or indirectly, by it, or by any of its Affiliates or Associates (including without limitation all Shares beneficially owned as of the respective record dates for the 2014 Annual Meeting and as of the record dates for any special meeting of shareholders) over which it exercises or has voting authority, to be present for quorum purposes and to be voted, at such meetings or at any adjournments or postponements thereof, in favor of the current members of the Board (including the JANA Nominees) that will be up for election at such meetings, and not to submit any proposal for consideration at, or bring any other business before, the 2014 Annual Meeting or initiate, encourage or participate in any "withhold" or similar campaign with respect to the election of directors at the 2014 Annual Meeting and shall not permit any of its Affiliates or Associates to do any of the foregoing or publicly or privately encourage or support any other stockholder to take any such actions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'beneficially owned' to 'nominally owned' regarding voting rights contradicts the concept of beneficial ownership, which defines who can actually vote shares. This creates confusion about voting obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 13d-3 defines beneficial ownership in terms of voting power or investment power. 'Nominal ownership' suggests a lack of real control or benefit, contradicting the SEC's focus on who truly controls the shares for voting and disclosure purposes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.13d-3 - Determination of beneficial owner. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-3 - Determination of beneficial owner. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-3 Determination of beneficial owner . (a) For the purposes of sections 13(d) and 13(g) of the Act a beneficial owner of a security includes any person who, directly or indirectly, through any contract, arrangement, understanding, relationship, or otherwise has or shares: (1) Voting power which includes the power to vote, or to direct the voting of, such security ; and/or, (2) Investment power which includes the power to dispose, or to direct the disposition of, such security . (b) Any person who, directly or indirectly, creates or uses a trust, proxy , power of attorney, pooling arrangement or any other contract, arrangement, or device with the purpose of effect of divesting such person of beneficial ownership of a security or preventing the vesting of such beneficial ownership as part of a plan or scheme to evade the reporting requirements of section 13(d) or (g) of the Act shall be deemed for purposes of such sections to be the beneficial owner of such security . (c) All securities of the same class beneficially owned by a person, regardless of the form which such beneficial ownership takes, shall be aggregated in calculating the number of shares beneficially owned by such person. (d) Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraphs (a) and (c) of this rule: (1) (i) A person shall be deemed to be the beneficial owner of a security , subject to the provisions of paragraph (b) of this rule, if that person has the right to acquire beneficial ownership of such security , as defined in Rule 13d-3(a) ( § 240.13d-3(a) ) within sixty days, including but not limited to any right to acquire: (A) Through the exercise of any option, warrant or right; (B) through the conversion of a se
perturbation.justification: The change from 'beneficially owned' to 'nominally owned' creates a legal contradiction. SEC Rule 13d-3, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines a 'beneficial owner' as someone who has voting power or investment power over a security. 'Nominal ownership' implies ownership in name only, without the associated voting or investment power. The original contract ensured that JANA would vote shares they effectively controlled, while the changed version could be interpreted to exclude shares they control but do not technically 'own', thus contradicting the definition of beneficial ownership by potentially allowing them to avoid their voting obligations as per the contract. This undermines the intention of ensuring JANA votes shares it controls in a specified manner.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement: (a) the terms "Affiliate" and "Associate" shall have the respective meanings set forth in Rule 12b­2 promulgated by the SEC under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"); (b) the terms "beneficial owner" and "beneficially own" shall have the same meanings as set forth in Rule 13d­3 promulgated by the SEC under the Exchange Act except that a person shall also be deemed to be the beneficial owner of all Shares which such person has the right to acquire (whether such right is exercisable immediately or only after the passage of time) pursuant to the exercise of any rights in connection with any securities or any agreement, arrangement or understanding (whether or not in writing), regardless of when such rights may be exercised and whether they are conditional, and all Shares which such person or any of such person's Affiliates or Associates has or shares the right to vote or dispose; and (c) the terms "person" or "persons" shall mean any individual, corporation (including not­for­profit), general or limited partnership, limited liability or unlimited liability company, joint venture, estate, trust, association, organization or other entity of any kind or nature.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement: (a) the terms "Affiliate" and "Associate" shall have the respective meanings set forth in Rule 12b­2 promulgated by the SEC under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"); (b) the terms "beneficial owner" and "beneficially own" shall not reflect the meanings as set forth in Rule 13d­3 promulgated by the SEC under the Exchange Act; instead, a beneficial owner is simply someone whose name is on the stock certificate; and (c) the terms "person" or "persons" shall mean any individual, corporation (including not­for­profit), general or limited partnership, limited liability or unlimited liability company, joint venture, estate, trust, association, organization or other entity of any kind or nature.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining 'beneficial owner' to only mean the name on the stock certificate directly contradicts the SEC's broad definition, which considers voting and investment power, not just record ownership. This could lead to misreporting and non-compliance with securities laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification disregards the SEC's established criteria for determining beneficial ownership, which includes indirect control and the power to vote or dispose of shares. Limiting it to the name on the certificate ignores the reality of nominee accounts and other forms of indirect ownership.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.13d-3 - Determination of beneficial owner. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-3 - Determination of beneficial owner. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-3 Determination of beneficial owner . (a) For the purposes of sections 13(d) and 13(g) of the Act a beneficial owner of a security includes any person who, directly or indirectly, through any contract, arrangement, understanding, relationship, or otherwise has or shares: (1) Voting power which includes the power to vote, or to direct the voting of, such security ; and/or, (2) Investment power which includes the power to dispose, or to direct the disposition of, such security . (b) Any person who, directly or indirectly, creates or uses a trust, proxy , power of attorney, pooling arrangement or any other contract, arrangement, or device with the purpose of effect of divesting such person of beneficial ownership of a security or preventing the vesting of such beneficial ownership as part of a plan or scheme to evade the reporting requirements of section 13(d) or (g) of the Act shall be deemed for purposes of such sections to be the beneficial owner of such security . (c) All securities of the same class beneficially owned by a person, regardless of the form which such beneficial ownership takes, shall be aggregated in calculating the number of shares beneficially owned by such person. (d) Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraphs (a) and (c) of this rule: (1) (i) A person shall be deemed to be the beneficial owner of a security , subject to the provisions of paragraph (b) of this rule, if that person has the right to acquire beneficial ownership of such security , as defined in Rule 13d-3(a) ( § 240.13d-3(a) ) within sixty days, including but not limited to any right to acquire: (A) Through the exercise of any option, warrant or right; (B) through the conversion of a se
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "beneficial owner" in a way that directly contradicts the definition provided in 17 CFR § 240.13d-3. The original contract adheres to the SEC's definition, while the changed text limits it to the name on the stock certificate. This contradicts the law, which states that a beneficial owner includes any person who, directly or indirectly, has voting or investment power over the security, regardless of whether their name is on the certificate. Specifically, 17 CFR § 240.13d-3(a) states that a beneficial owner includes any person who has "(1) Voting power which includes the power to vote, or to direct the voting of, such security; and/or, (2) Investment power which includes the power to dispose, or to direct the disposition of, such security."
